common_name: Bob Gordon Elderberry (Kansas State University Very Large Fruit Elderberry)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; plant at least two different elderberry cultivars for cross-pollination — Bob Gordon is self-fertile but produces significantly larger crops with a pollinator cultivar planted nearby.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Bob Gordon was selected by James Millican from a wild elderberry population in Kansas and released through Kansas State University — it produces some of the largest individual berries of any American elderberry cultivar, with berry diameter significantly exceeding the standard wild-type, and heavy, pendant clusters of dark purple-black berries that ripen in late summer. American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) is a vigorously suckering native shrub producing flat-topped, white flower clusters (cymes) in June–July — large, fragrant, and excellent for elderflower cordials, vinegars, and baked goods — followed by heavy clusters of dark purple-black berries in August–September used for elderberry syrup, wine, jelly, and juice. 'Bob Gordon' performs well in the humid heat of Gulf South zone 8-9 conditions, though elderberries prefer the slightly cooler and more reliably moist soils of zone 7-8 for maximum production. In zone 9, provide consistent moisture and afternoon shade protection to maintain health through summer. The very large berry size and heavy production make Bob Gordon one of the top choices for home and commercial elderberry production in the eastern US. Plants spread by suckers to form colonies — remove suckers regularly to maintain a well-defined shrub form.

common_name: Nova Elderberry (Early-Ripening High-Yield Canadian Elderberry Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Nova is a good pollinator for Bob Gordon and Adams cultivars — planting two or more different cultivars consistently increases fruit production.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Nova is a Canadian elderberry cultivar developed by Agriculture Canada that produces large, dense, heavy berry clusters ripening one of the earliest in the American elderberry season — typically 7–10 days earlier than Adams and other mid-season cultivars. The large, heavy clusters of very dark purple-black berries — dense and pendant when fully ripe — combine good fresh eating quality with excellent processing characteristics for elderberry syrup, juice, wine, and jelly production. Nova produces abundant flat-topped flower clusters in June–July, providing one of the most productive native pollinator resources in the early summer garden — elder flowers are visited by extraordinary numbers of native bees, small native bees, and beneficial insects that use the pollen and nectar. The compact cyme structure of Nova clusters makes harvesting somewhat easier than looser-clustered varieties. Nova's combination of early ripening, high production, and compact plant habit (slightly smaller than some other cultivars at 5–10 feet) makes it a practical choice for home gardens. As with all American elderberry, Nova spreads by root suckers to form colonies — plan for the suckering habit and remove suckers regularly to maintain the desired planting footprint.

common_name: Sundial Mango Portulaca (AAS-Winning Warm Mango-Orange Moss Rose for Summer Heat)

planting.note: Surface-sow seeds after the last frost in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; do not cover seeds — portulaca seeds require light for germination; Sundial Mango blooms reliably even in cloudy conditions unlike older portulaca varieties that closed their flowers on overcast days.

directions: Surface-sow seeds on well-drained, lean to sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sundial Mango is an All-America Selections award-winning portulaca (moss rose) — part of the Sundial series selected for an important improvement over older portulaca varieties: the ability to remain open and in bloom on overcast days. Traditional portulaca varieties close their flowers in shade, cloudy weather, and in the afternoon, significantly limiting their ornamental value to only the sunniest morning hours. The Sundial series maintains flower opening throughout the day in typical light conditions, including partial overcast. The Mango color — warm, sunny, deep apricot-orange to mango-orange — is one of the richest and most distinctive in the Sundial color range, producing a warm, vibrant display in the rock garden, gravel mulch, and dry border positions where portulaca excels. Portulaca is one of the most truly drought-tolerant and heat-resistant summer annuals for full-sun Gulf South positions — thriving in lean, sandy, or gravelly soil where other annuals would fail, and continuing to bloom through the driest and hottest periods of Gulf South summer. The succulent stems and leaves store water to bridge dry periods without wilting. The low, spreading mounding habit (6–8 inches tall, 12–18 inches wide) makes portulaca ideal for rock walls, gravel gardens, and the fronts of full-sun borders.

common_name: Fresh Look Red Celosia (AAS-Winning Compact Vivid-Red Cockscomb Celosia)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting or direct sow after last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fresh Look Red is a crested (cockscomb) type celosia — the ruffled, velvety, brain-like flower heads are distinctive and maintain their vivid color through the entire warm season without fading.

directions: Transplant after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fresh Look Red is an All-America Selections award-winning celosia in the crested (cockscomb) type — producing the distinctive velvety, ruffled, brain-shaped flower heads in vivid crimson-red. The compact, uniform habit (12–18 inches) with large, well-developed flower heads makes Fresh Look Red more ornamental and practical for garden use than older cockscomb types that were often taller and less uniformly sized. The velvety texture of the crested flower head is unique in summer annuals — the crimped, corrugated surface catches light at different angles to create depth and luminosity in the vivid red color that plain-surfaced flowers cannot match. The color maintains its intensity through the entire warm season without bleaching or browning — one of the few truly summer-proof vivid-red annuals for Gulf South gardens where July–August heat causes many flowers to fade or develop heat-stress brown tones. The compact size and vivid color make Fresh Look Red outstanding for mixed container plantings, border edging, and mass plantings in full-sun summer beds. The flower heads dry well for winter arrangements, maintaining their deep red color for months when dried quickly in a warm, low-humidity location.

common_name: Artillery Fern (Tropical Pilea Groundcover Mimicking Fine-Textured Fern Foliage)

planting.note: Plant container transplants after the last frost in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade; Artillery Fern's name comes from its ability to explosively discharge pollen in small visible clouds when the tiny flowers are touched or disturbed by insects or wind — a harmless spectacle in the garden.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade; Artillery Fern is not a fern at all — it is a succulent-looking tropical groundcover plant (Pilea microphylla, family Urticaceae) that mimics the fine-textured, fernlike appearance of true ferns with its tiny, densely overlapping, bright green succulent leaves on delicate, branching stems. The very small, succulent-textured leaves (2–4mm) are arranged in dense overlapping rows along the stems, creating a texture so fine-grained and bright-green that it closely resembles some delicate maidenhair fern fronds at a distance. The common name 'artillery fern' comes from the plant's unusual reproductive behavior: when the tiny, inconspicuous flowers are ripe, they explosively discharge pollen in small visible clouds when disturbed — a fascinating botanical defense mechanism visible on close inspection on warm, humid days. In zone 9, Artillery Fern is grown as a warm-season annual for shaded beds, container underplanting, and terrace pots — it thrives through Gulf South summer humidity and heat in partial to full shade where it provides lush, fine-textured green coverage from spring through frost. The succulent tissue tolerates brief dry periods better than true ferns. Self-sows in protected positions and may overwinter in zone 9b. Outstanding as a groundcover beneath shade containers and as a fine-textured companion for bold-leaved shade tropicals.
